notes: Hebrew ayin covers both Arabic ʿayn (pharyngeal) and ghayn (velar fricative); the ghayn-derived reflex is indistinguishable in writing and modern pronunciation
Hebrew het covers both Arabic ḥa (pharyngeal fricative) and kha (velar fricative); Classical Hebrew may have preserved the distinction but it is fully merged in modern Hebrew
Hebrew dalet covers both Arabic dal (plain stop) and dhal (interdental fricative); Hebrew lost the fricative variant entirely
Hebrew tav covers Arabic ta (plain dental) though tet (ט) covers the emphatic ṭa; the emphatic-plain distinction is partially preserved via a different letter but tha/ta are still merged
Arabic tha (interdental fricative) regularly corresponds to Hebrew shin (שׁ); e.g. Arabic ثلاثة → Hebrew שלוש; not a merger of two Arabic sounds but a cross-language correspondence
Hebrew tsade covers both Arabic emphatic sad (ص) and emphatic dad (ض); dad was historically a lateral emphatic and its exact Proto-Semitic reflex is debated but both collapse to tsade in Hebrew

Arabic emphatic dha (ظ) has no direct Hebrew equivalent; it corresponds variously to tsade (צ) or zayin (ז) depending on the root; reflects the historical dad/dha merger in Proto-Semitic reconstruction

Hebrew preserves a shin/sin distinction (שׁ vs שׂ) but it does not map cleanly to Arabic shin/sin; Arabic sin (س) typically → Hebrew sin (שׂ) or samekh (ס), Arabic shin (ش) → Hebrew shin (שׁ); not a simple merger but a realignment
Arabic qaf (ق) maps to Hebrew qof (ק), not a merger; Arabic kaf (ك) maps to Hebrew kaf (כ); the distinction is preserved
Aramaic ayin covers both Arabic ʿayn and ghayn, parallel to the Hebrew merger; same script, same convergence
Aramaic het covers both Arabic ḥa and kha; the pharyngeal-velar distinction collapsed as in Hebrew
Aramaic dalet covers Arabic dal; Arabic dhal (ذ) has no separate Aramaic letter; merged to dalet as in Hebrew
Aramaic taw covers Arabic ta; the emphatic ṭa maps to Aramaic tet (ט); pattern matches Hebrew
Aramaic tsade covers both Arabic emphatic sad and emphatic dad; identical merger to Hebrew
Aramaic typically maps Arabic tha to taw (ת) rather than shin, differing from Hebrew; e.g. three: Arabic ثلاثة vs Aramaic תלתא; this is a key Aramaic-specific correspondence
Aramaic maps Arabic emphatic dha (ظ) to tet (ט) more consistently than Hebrew does; this is an Aramaic-specific reflex
Ancient Greek has no pharyngeal consonants; Arabic ʿayn (ع) is dropped entirely in Greek borrowings or represented by a vowel onset; e.g. Arabic عنبر → Greek ἄμβαρ (ambar)
Arabic ghayn (غ) is lost in Greek; occasionally rendered as γ (gamma) in loanwords but typically dropped or vocalized
Arabic ḥa (pharyngeal fricative) sometimes maps to Greek chi (χ) in early borrowings but is frequently dropped; e.g. some Semitic roots with ح appear in Greek without initial consonant
Arabic kha (velar fricative) maps reasonably to Greek chi (χ), which was originally an aspirated stop [kh]; this is the most consistent Semitic-Greek consonant correspondence for these sounds
Both Arabic ḥa and kha collapse to Greek chi (χ) or are dropped; the pharyngeal-velar distinction is entirely lost in Greek
Arabic qaf (uvular stop) maps to Greek kappa (κ); Greek has no uvular series so the uvular-velar distinction collapses; e.g. Arabic قانون → Greek κανών (canon)
Arabic emphatic ṭa maps to Greek tau (τ) or theta (θ); the emphatic quality is lost; e.g. Arabic طين → Greek τεῖνος; Greek theta (θ) is an aspirated dental not a pharyngealized one
Arabic emphatic sad (ص) maps to Greek sigma (σ); the emphatic pharyngealization is lost entirely
Arabic emphatic dad (ض) maps variably to Greek delta (δ) or sigma (σ); the emphatic quality is lost
Arabic tha (interdental fricative) maps to Greek theta (θ); this is a strong correspondence in Semitic-Greek cognate pairs; e.g. Proto-Semitic interdentals → Greek aspirates
Arabic dhal (voiced interdental fricative) maps to Greek delta (δ); the fricative quality is lost in favor of a plain stop
Arabic emphatic dha (ظ) has no Greek equivalent; maps variably; the distinction from ذ and ض is lost entirely
Latin has no pharyngeal consonants; Arabic ʿayn is dropped or becomes a vowel in Latin borrowings; e.g. Arabic عرق → Latin arak (with vowel onset)
Arabic ghayn drops in Latin or occasionally maps to g; no systematic correspondence

Arabic ḥa maps to Latin h or is dropped; Latin h was largely silent in Classical Latin so even this mapping loses phonetic weight; e.g. Arabic حمار → Latin asinus (unrelated root) vs direct borrowings with initial h
Arabic kha maps to Latin h or is dropped; the velar fricative has no Latin equivalent; both ح and خ collapse to h or zero
Both Arabic ḥa and kha collapse to Latin h (or are dropped); pharyngeal-velar distinction entirely lost
Arabic qaf (uvular stop) maps to Latin c/qu; the uvular position is lost in favor of velar; e.g. Arabic قيثارة → Latin cithara; Arabic قرطاس → Latin charta
Arabic emphatic ṭa maps to Latin t; the pharyngealization/emphasis is entirely lost; e.g. Arabic طين (clay) → Latin terra (via PIE, not direct borrowing)
Arabic emphatic sad maps to Latin s; the emphatic quality is lost; e.g. Arabic صفر → Medieval Latin cifra → English cipher
Arabic emphatic dad maps to Latin d; the emphatic quality is lost
Arabic tha (interdental fricative) maps variably to Latin t, s, or f depending on borrowing path and period; no single systematic correspondent
Arabic dhal maps to Latin d; the fricative quality is lost in favor of a plain stop
Arabic emphatic dha has no Latin equivalent; maps variably; all emphasis distinctions lost
English has no pharyngeal consonants; Arabic ʿayn is lost in all borrowing paths (direct Arabic → English, Arabic → Medieval Latin → English, proto-Semitic → proto-IE → Germanic → English)
Arabic ghayn is lost in English; occasionally g in very early borrowings via Arabic → Latin path
Arabic ḥa maps to English h or is dropped; via Latin path h was often already lost, making English reflexes vowel-initial; borrowing path matters significantly
Arabic kha maps to English h, k, or is dropped; via Greek path it may appear as ch (e.g. chemistry from Arabic كيمياء via Greek); via Latin path as h or dropped
Arabic qaf maps to English c, k, or qu depending on borrowing path; via Latin: c/qu (e.g. Arabic قيثارة → English guitar via Spanish); via direct Arabic → English: often k
Arabic emphatic ṭa maps to English t; all emphatic quality lost across all borrowing paths; e.g. Arabic طرف → English tariff via Arabic → Italian → English
Arabic emphatic sad maps to English s, z, or c depending on path; e.g. Arabic صفر → English zero via Arabic → Medieval Latin cifra → Italian zefiro; or cipher via different Latin path
Arabic emphatic dad maps to English d; emphatic quality lost across all paths

Arabic tha maps to English th (in direct borrowings), t (via Latin), or s (via Greek); English retains the interdental fricative th so direct Arabic → English loans may preserve it, but via Latin/Greek the mapping shifts
Arabic dhal maps to English d or th; English has voiced interdental th (as in 'the') which could match dhal, but most borrowings arrive via Latin/French where it has already become d
Arabic emphatic dha has no English equivalent; all emphasis and the sound itself is lost across all borrowing paths

Arabic jim maps to English j, g (soft), or y depending on borrowing path and period; e.g. Arabic جبر → English algebra via Arabic al-jabr → Medieval Latin; Arabic جيراف → English giraffe via Italian giraffa

Persian script preserves both Arabic ʿayn (ع) and ghayn (غ) as distinct letters in Arabic loanwords; however in colloquial Persian pronunciation both are often realized as a glottal stop or dropped; the orthographic distinction is maintained even when phonemic distinction is lost in speech

Persian preserves Arabic ḥa (ح) in script for Arabic loanwords but in native Persian pronunciation both ح and ه are realized as [h]; the script distinction is maintained but the phonemic distinction collapses to h in speech

Persian preserves both ح and خ as distinct letters; خ retains a velar fricative pronunciation [x] in Persian (which also has this sound natively); ح is pronounced [h]; the distinction is partially preserved in pronunciation unlike in Hebrew

Persian has three letters for s-sounds from Arabic (ث ث س ص) but all are pronounced [s] in Persian; the Arabic emphatic-plain and interdental-sibilant distinctions are entirely lost in Persian pronunciation; the script distinction is preserved for Arabic loans

Persian has four letters for z-sounds from Arabic (ذ ز ض ظ) but all are pronounced [z] in Persian; this is the famous 'four z's' of Persian script; all Arabic distinctions (emphatic, fricative, interdental) collapse to a single [z]

Persian preserves both ت (ta) and ط (tta emphatic) in script for Arabic loans; both are pronounced [t] in Persian; the emphatic distinction is lost in pronunciation but the orthographic distinction remains

Persian preserves Arabic qaf (ق) both in script and in pronunciation; Persian has the uvular stop natively (though in colloquial speech it often shifts to glottal stop); the letter is maintained without merger

In Persian pronunciation, Arabic ʿayn (ع) at word boundaries is realized as a glottal stop or long vowel onset; in colloquial speech it often drops entirely; the script form ع is always preserved for Arabic loans

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic jim (ج) ↔ K/C/G in English and Germanic languages. Examples: جليد (jaleed, ice) → cold (initial K); جمل (jamal, camel) → camel; also attested in German Kamel = جمل. The jim → velar shift is systematic across early Semitic-Germanic contact.

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic ba (ب) ↔ F in English/Germanic. Example: بنصر (binsir, finger) → finger. The b/f interchange is a documented bilabial–labiodental alternation recurring across Semitic and Germanic branches.

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic dal (د) ↔ T in English/Germanic. Example: دنّ (dann, barrel) → tunnel / tonne. The d→t shift (voiced-to-voiceless stop) is one of the most common cross-lingual consonant correspondences, paralleling Grimm's Law voicing shifts.

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic qaf (ق) ↔ C/K in Latin and English, with full borrowing chains documented. Examples: قطين (qatin, shackled) → catena (Latin) → chain (English); قناة (qanah, channel) → canna (Latin) → canyon (English); قنديل (qindeel, lamp) → candela (Latin) → chandelier (French/English). Extends the existing ق→c/k entry with concrete multi-step chains.

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic ayin (ع) ↔ vowel or dropped in English. Example: عمّ (amm, uncle) → eam (Old English) → uncle. The guttural pharyngeal is lost entirely and replaced by a vowel onset; the remaining consonant skeleton shifts (amm → eam → uncle via nasalization and suffix). Consistent with the general ع→∅/V mapping already documented for English.

Beyond the Word shift: Arabic fa (ف) ↔ B/BH/PH in PIE languages. Example: فاه (to utter/speak) ↔ PIE *bha- (to speak) → Latin fama → English famous. The bilabial fricative f alternates with the labial stop b and aspirate bh across the Semitic-IE interface; this is a documented Proto-Semitic ↔ PIE root correspondence.

Guttural collapse (mined, 861 pairs): pharyngeal ayin (ع) and pharyngeal fricative ha (ح) both yield h or deletion in English. Group frequency 171/193 (weight 0.886); dominant reflex is deletion (129 obs), h-retention secondary (25 obs).
Guttural collapse (mined): pharyngeal ayin (ع) and velar kha (خ) both lose their guttural quality in English; ع → ∅/V, خ → h/k/∅. Part of the 4-letter guttural group (weight 0.886, 193 obs).

Guttural collapse (mined): pharyngeal ha (ح) and velar kha (خ) both map to English h or are dropped. Group weight 0.886 (171/193 obs). Extends existing Latin/Greek rules to English with mined frequency support.

Guttural collapse (mined): pharyngeal ha (ح) → h/∅ and ghayin (غ) → ∅/g in English; both gutturals collapse with deletion as dominant reflex (129/193 obs). Part of 4-letter guttural group, weight 0.886.
Guttural collapse (mined): velar kha (خ) and velar ghayin (غ) both reduce in English; kha → h/k/∅, ghayin → ∅/g. Part of 4-letter guttural group, weight 0.886 (171/193 obs).
Emphatic dental collapse (mined, 861 pairs): emphatic ṭa (ط) and plain ta (ت) both map to English t; emphatic pharyngealization entirely lost. t dominant (102/175 obs), weight 0.754.
Emphatic dental collapse (mined): emphatic ṭa (ط) → t and voiced interdental dhal (ذ) → d/th in English; both lose emphatic/fricative quality. Weight 0.754 (132/175 obs). Dominant reflex: t.
Emphatic dental collapse (mined): both emphatic ṭa (ط) and emphatic dha (ظ) map to t/d in English with total loss of pharyngealization. Part of 4-letter dental group, weight 0.754.
Emphatic dental collapse (mined): plain ta (ت) → t and voiced interdental dhal (ذ) → d/th merge to the t/d region in English. Weight 0.754 (132/175 obs).
Emphatic dental collapse (mined): plain ta (ت) and emphatic dha (ظ) both collapse to t in English. Part of 4-letter dental merger, weight 0.754.

Emphatic dental collapse (mined): voiced interdental dhal (ذ) and emphatic dha (ظ) both reduce to d/z in English with loss of fricative and emphatic features. Part of 4-letter dental merger, weight 0.754.

Velar/uvular merger (mined, 861 pairs): uvular qaf (ق) and velar kaf (ك) both map to English k/c; the uvular-velar distinction is entirely lost. c dominant (93 obs), g secondary (65), k tertiary (17). Group weight 0.727 (221/304 obs).

Velar/uvular merger (mined): velar kaf (ك) and palatal jim (ج) both fall in the k/c/g region in English. Jim → g/j, kaf → k/c; the palatal-velar distinction collapses. Part of 3-letter velar merger, weight 0.727.

Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined, 861 pairs): emphatic sad (ص) and plain sin (س) both map to English s; emphatic pharyngealization lost. s dominant (129/259 obs), weight 0.707. Extends existing entry with mined frequency data.
Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined): emphatic sad (ص) → s/c and zayin (ز) → z/s in English; both collapse to the s/z region. Group weight 0.707 (183/259 obs).
Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined): emphatic sad (ص) and shin (ش) both reduce to s/c in English with loss of emphatic and palatal features. Part of 4-letter sibilant group, weight 0.707.
Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined): plain sin (س) and zayin (ز) both map to s/z in English; the voicing distinction is often neutralized. Part of 4-letter sibilant group, weight 0.707 (183/259 obs).
Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined): plain sin (س) and shin (ش) both reduce to s in English; the palatal feature of shin is lost. Part of 4-letter sibilant group, weight 0.707.
Emphatic sibilant collapse (mined): zayin (ز) → z/s and shin (ش) → s/sh in English; both fall in the sibilant region. Part of 4-letter sibilant group, weight 0.707 (183/259 obs).

Labial group merger (mined, 861 pairs): ba (ب) and mim (م) both fall in the labial region in English; ba → b/f/p, mim → m/n. The labial stop and nasal are related via bilabial place of articulation. Group weight 0.649 (386/595 obs).
Labial group merger (mined): ba (ب) and waw (و) both occupy the bilabial/labiodental zone; ba → b/p/f, waw → v/w/b in English. Part of 4-letter labial group, weight 0.649.
Labial group merger (mined): fa (ف) and mim (م) both map to English labials; fa → f/p/b, mim → m. Bilabial–labiodental alternation in labial group, weight 0.649 (386/595 obs).
Labial group merger (mined): fa (ف) → f/p and waw (و) → v/w/f in English; the labial fricatives interchange. Part of 4-letter labial group, weight 0.649.

Labial group merger (mined): mim (م) and waw (و) both occupy the bilabial region; mim → m, waw → v/w. The nasal-approximant alternation is attested in labial weakening environments. Part of 4-letter labial group, weight 0.649 (386/595 obs).
